Entity_Description: Mount Currie is a community in the Sea-to-Sky region of British Columbia, Canada, roughly 6 km east of Pemberton on the floor of the Pemberton Valley and about 119 km north of Vancouver. It is the principal community of the Lil'wat Nation (Líl̓wat), a St'át'imc people, and one of the larger First Nations communities in the province, with an estimated population near 1,242. Named for the mountain that rises above it, the community sits along Highway 99 where the paved corridor becomes the Duffey Lake Road. Farm properties, band housing, and the upper village of Xit'olacw make up its building stock.

Homes here range from farm properties on the valley floor to band housing and the upper village of Xit'olacw, all built to handle the deep winter snow that settles beneath the peak the community is named for. {COMPANY_NAME} brings {SERVICE} to this stretch of the Sea-to-Sky corridor, pairing {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} with real knowledge of local conditions. Whether you own an older valley home or a newer build up the hillside, our {BUSINESS_TYPE} handles it. For {ESTIMATE_TYPE}, call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Locals know Mount Currie as the home of the Lil'wat Nation, a community set on the floor of the Pemberton Valley where the Coast Mountains close in around Highway 99. Just six kilometres east of Pemberton, it marks the spot where the paved corridor turns into the Duffey Lake Road, the long mountain route toward Lillooet. That geography shapes everything a {BUSINESS_TYPE} needs to know here. Winters drop deep, wet snow across the valley, and the freeze-thaw swings common to the Sea-to-Sky corridor test rooflines, foundations, and exterior finishes season after season.

The building stock is varied. Older farm properties spread across the valley floor, band housing lines the community core, and the upper village of Xit'olacw climbs the hillside above the river, where exposure and elevation change how snow and wind hit a structure. {COMPANY_NAME} accounts for those differences instead of treating every address the same. A valley farmhouse near the Lillooet River and a newer home up the slope call for different {SERVICE} approaches, and our {BUSINESS_TYPE} plans accordingly.

The economy of this stretch of the valley runs on farming, the Lil'wat Nation itself as a major local employer, and the four-season tourism that flows up the Sea-to-Sky corridor from Whistler and Pemberton. That mix means we work on everything from working agricultural buildings to family homes and rental properties. {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION}

Because Mount Currie sits at the far end of the serviced Pemberton Valley, response time matters more here than in town. We cover the community and its neighbours - Pemberton just up the road, Whistler about 28 kilometres south, and the wider Squamish-Lillooet Regional District - so a crew is rarely far away. {COMPANY_NAME} offers {RESPONSE_TIME} response and {ESTIMATE_TYPE}, backed by {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}. From the first snow beneath Mount Currie peak to the dry heat of late summer, {SERVICE_LIST} keep valley homes and buildings protected. When you need {SERVICE} you can count on in Mount Currie, our team knows the ground, the weather, and the drive. Call {PHONE} to get started.
Local_Facts:
- Mount Currie is the principal community of the Lil'wat Nation (Líl̓wat), a St'át'imc people.
- It sits about 6 km east of Pemberton in the Sea-to-Sky region, roughly 119 km north of Vancouver.
- The estimated population is around 1,242 (GeoNames estimate; approximate).
- The community is named after Mount Currie, the mountain rising above the Pemberton Valley.
- Highway 99 passes through the community and continues east as the Duffey Lake Road toward Lillooet.
- The community includes an upper village, Xit'olacw, built on the hillside above the valley floor.
- Winters bring heavy snow loads and freeze-thaw typical of the Coast Mountains corridor.
